coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots derived from folic acid as an ultra-succinct smart antimicrobial nanosystem for selective killing of S. aureus and biofilm eradication

Lu, Haojie
Li, Peili
Huang, Ruobing
Xu, Chunning
Gong, Xuedong
Xiao, Yuhong
Xing, Xiaodong
coredata.dc:description: Developing efficient and safe approach to counteract the bacterial infections has become an urgent clinical challenge. In this work, we report folic acid derived carbon dots (FA-CDs) prepared by a one-step pyrolysis method. The FA-CDs can respond to the environmental stimuli of bacterial infections and acquire antimicrobial capacity “on demand” as a simple and effective antimicrobial nanosystem. The assays on antibacterial properties and mechanism show that FA-CDs are able to induce the gradual increase of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels in bacteria under the simulated acidic environment and display pH-responsive biocidal activity. In a neutral condition, FA-CDs have no antibacterial activity, which will be conducive to mitigating the rise of bacterial drug resistance. The responsive antibacterial behavior can be attributed to that FA-CDs exhibit distinctive oxidase-like and peroxidase-like activity under acidic stimuli and inhibit the superoxide dismutase activity in bacteria cells, causing the accumulation of intracellular ROS. Meanwhile, FA-CDs can selectively kill Gram-positive bacterium S. aureus and remove mature biofilms. Besides, the in vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrate that FA-CDs possess excellent biocompatibility, and show remarkable therapeutic effect on the S. aureus-infected back wound models of mouse, which are crucial for clinical applications. Thus, this study highlights the potential application of FA-CDs as an ultra-succinct smart antimicrobial nanosystem for coping with infections caused by S. aureus in the clinical setting, and will bring about a new perspective on developing antimicrobial CDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots derived from folic acid as an ultra-succinct smart antimicrobial nanosystem for selective killing of S. aureus and biofilm eradication

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing efficient and safe approach to counteract the bacterial infections has become an urgent clinical challenge. In this work, we report folic acid derived carbon dots (FA-CDs) prepared by a one-step pyrolysis method. The FA-CDs can respond to the environmental stimuli of bacterial infections and acquire antimicrobial capacity “on demand” as a simple and effective antimicrobial nanosystem. The assays on antibacterial properties and mechanism show that FA-CDs are able to induce the gradual increase of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels in bacteria under the simulated acidic environment and display pH-responsive biocidal activity. In a neutral condition, FA-CDs have no antibacterial activity, which will be conducive to mitigating the rise of bacterial drug resistance. The responsive antibacterial behavior can be attributed to that FA-CDs exhibit distinctive oxidase-like and peroxidase-like activity under acidic stimuli and inhibit the superoxide dismutase activity in bacteria cells, causing the accumulation of intracellular ROS. Meanwhile, FA-CDs can selectively kill Gram-positive bacterium S. aureus and remove mature biofilms. Besides, the in vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrate that FA-CDs possess excellent biocompatibility, and show remarkable therapeutic effect on the S. aureus-infected back wound models of mouse, which are crucial for clinical applications. Thus, this study highlights the potential application of FA-CDs as an ultra-succinct smart antimicrobial nanosystem for coping with infections caused by S. aureus in the clinical setting, and will bring about a new perspective on developing antimicrobial CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial biofilms are the inhomogeneous membrane-like matters formed when bacteria secrete large amounts of extracellular polymeric substancese (EPS) to adapt to the surrounding microenvironment and wrap the bacterium itself in them [1,2]. The matrix of EPS acts as a physical barrier for bacteria, highly resisting the penetration of antibiotics and other small-molecule antimicrobial agents into the biofilm [3–8]. It is estimated that up to 80% of human bacterial infections are intertwined with biofilms, which has become an increasingly important clinical problem worldwide [9]. With conventional strategies like antibiotic treatment, bacteria inside the biofilms are inordinately difficult to completely remove and may develop drug resistance, leading to unnecessary side effects on patients [10–12]. Therefore, it is of great significance to find effective and safe solutions to the intractable threat from bacterial biofilms.

It is well known that the acidic metabolites secreted by bacteria which accumulate inside the biofilm, result in a significant decrease in pH at the infected sites [13–17]; also, the infected sites possess some special features, such as the over-expression of hyaluronidase, phosphaholipase, toxin, folate receptor, etc. [18–20] These environmental and physiological factors provide an opportunity for the fabrication of smart drug delivery systems. In recent years, researchers have constructed a series of novel nanosystems by incorporating stimulus-response strategies into the antimicrobial carriers, which can respond to environmental stimuli at the infection sites, such as the acidic environment [14–17] and over-expressed enzymes [21,22], and acquire antibacterial ability “on demand”. These advanced systems achieve effective biofilm removal while improving the utilization of antimicrobial agents, reducing the toxicity to normal human tissues, and solving the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria [23]. For example, Xie et al. recently constructed pH-responsive Ag nanoparticle clusters using functional polymers containing ortho ester segment wrapped around Ag nanoparticles (NPs) [24]. When exposed to the acidic microenvironment, the encapsulated layer collapsed and released Ag NPs. However, these responsive antibacterial nanosystems are greatly limited to their tedious preparation procedures and not suitable for large-scale applications. In addition, they are also restricted by their low ambient stability, poor biocompatibility, and high cost, etc. Accordingly, it remains a major concern and daunting challenge to construct simple and effective antimicrobial nanosystems with responsiveness to the microenvironment of bacterial infections for practical biomedical applications.

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel carbon-based nanomaterial, are widely used in the fields of detectors [25,26], biosensors [27,28], drug delivery [29,30], bioimaging [31] and antibacterial therapy [32,33] due to their chemical stability, excellent optical properties and antimicrobial properties. Notably, some studies have manifested that nitrogen-doped CDs exhibits good antimicrobial activity, which produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) inside the bacterial cells through enzyme mimetic activities such as peroxidase-like and oxidase-like to kill bacteria in a short time [34–36]. Moreover, positively charged CDs could disrupt bacterial cytoplasmic membrane, resulting in death of bacteria [37,38]. These antibacterial CDs are less susceptible to drug resistance and also show good biocompatibility, making them a very promising alternative to antibiotics [32]. Recently, many in-depth studies have been conducted using carbon dots as antimicrobial components and combining them with responsive units to construct smart composite systems [14,15,39]. Our group fabricated nanoscale metal-organic frameworks (NMOFs) coencapsulating antibacterial CDs and folic acid (FA) [15]. FA, as the natural form of water-soluble vitamins that is essential to human body [40], can bind specifically to folate receptors overexpressed by bacterial biofilms, therefore, as the targeting moieties, to improve the antibiofilm efficiency [15]. However, similarly, these composite systems based on antibacterial CDs also suffer from tedious steps, low ambient stability and high cost. Hence, an ultra-succinct smart antimicrobial nanosystem based on CDs, which can kill bacteria effectively under stimuli control and eridacate bacterial biofilm, is urgently needed.

Herein, using FA as the precursor, we develop nitrogen-rich carbon dots (FA-CDs) through a simple one-step pyrolysis method. We are surprised to find that FA-CDs exhibit responsive antibacterial abilities in the acidic environment of the simulated infection site, and can effectively remove established biofilm; however, no obvious antibacterial activity is observed in a neutral environment, which suggest that sole FA-CDs can be used as a smart antimicrobial nanosystem with pH responsiveness. In addition, FA-CDs display selective antibacterial abilities for gram-positive S. aureus. A series of enzyme activity assays show that FA-CDs possess excellent oxidase-like and peroxidase-like activity in the mild acidic environment, and can inhibit the superoxide dismutase activity of S. aureus, causing the accumulation of intracellular ROS in bacteria cells. In vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrate that FA-CDs possess excellent biocompatibility, and show better therapeutic effect than antibiotics on the wound model of S. aureus infection.

Folic acid (FA,98%) was purchased from Bide Pharmatech Ltd(Shanghai, China). The strains of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 25923) and Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) were obtained from Nanjing Lezhen Biotechnology Ltd (Nanjing, China). Ascorbic acid (AA, 99%), 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein Diacetate (DCFH-DA, 97%) and 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 99%) were obtained from Aladdin. Total Superoxide Dismutase Assay Kit with NBT was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). The AO/EB Dual Fluorescence Staining Kit was bought from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Crystal violet (CV) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was bought from Dojindo (Kumamoto, Japan). Methanol and ethanol were acquired from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China) and used without further purification. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4 or 5.5) served to mimic physiological conditions or infection sites.

Referring to previous reports, FA-CDs were prepared by a simple one-step pyrolysis method using folic acid as the precursor [41,42]. Briefly, 0.1 g of folic acid powder was added to 5 mL of deionized water under ultrasound. The suspension were transferred into a ceramic crucible in a chamber furnace and further heated to certain temperature (250, 260, 270, 280, 290, 300, 310) at a rate of 4 °C/min and kept at this temperature for 3 h. After natural cooling, 10 mL of deionized water was added to the crucible and sonicated for 30 min. The turbid solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was taken to centrifuged again and the process was repeated 3–5 times. Then, the CDs solution was poured into dialysis bags (MWCO: 500 Da) and dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h. Finally, FA-CDs powder was obtained by vacuum freeze-drying.

The morphological characteristics of the samples were observed by a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL, JEM-2100) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The BI-90Plus Zeta potential analyzer (Brookhaven) was used to measure the surface potential and particle size distribution in the sample solution. In order to characterize the crystalline phases of samples, powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) were carried out on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) with a 2θ range of 20–80°. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectrometer (FL) were recorded by FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, Nicolet IS10), UV–visible spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, Biomate3S) and fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, F-7000), respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS, Shimadzu) was used to identify the elemental composition of the FA-CDs surfaces.

S. aureus (ATCC25923, Gram-positive) and E. coli (ATCC25922, Gram-negative) were incubated in Luria-Bertani medium (LB) at 37 °C for 18 h. All vessels and samples were autoclaved before each experiment.
1)
Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) test [43].

The sample solution (1 mg mL−1) was prepared by dispersing samples into Liquid LB medium (pH = 5.5, 6.5 or 7.4). From the initial concentration of the samples, a series of 1 mL sample solution were prepared using the two-fold dilution method. Then, 20 μL of S. aureus or E. coli solution (∼106 CFU mL−1) was added to each gradient tube and the tubes were incubated at 37 °C in a shaker for 24 h. The concentration of the CDs in the last clear test tube was the MIC value. All tests were performed in triplicate.
2)
Time-dynamic bactericidal assay [44].

FA-CDs (1 mg) were added to 1 mL of PBS (pH 7.4 or 5.5) containing 100 μL of S. aureus or E. coli (∼106 CFU mL−1), and the mixtures were allowed to incubate at 37 °C on a shaker. 100 μL of the mixtures were extracted after 1, 2, 4, 6 and 8 h of incubation, respectively, and then inoculated into solid LB medium. Colonies growing in solid LB medium were counted after 24 h. Control without samples was performed in the same way. Each test was performed in triplicate.
3)
SEM observation [32,33].

Bacterial suspension (100 μL, S.aureus or E. coli, ∼106 CFU mL−1) and FA-CDs (1 mL, FA-CD280, 1 mg mL−1) were incubated at 37 °C for 6 h and the PBS without CDs was used as the blank control. After incubation, the bacteria were treated with a PBS containing 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 24 h to fix them on the coverslips. Different concentrations of ethanol solutions (30, 50, 70, 80, 90, 95 and 100%) were used for gradient elution of the fixed bacteria. After drying at the critical point of carbon dioxide and sputtering coated with gold, the morphological changes of bacteria were observed using a scanning electron microscope.

In the biofilm culture, S. aureus (10 μL,∼106 CFU mL−1) were grown in 2 mL of 0.5% glucose-added Tryptic soy broth (TSB) in the 6-well microplates and a sterile coverslips was placed in it. The above inoculum was placed in the incubator for 48 h at 37 °C. Then, the biofilm formed was gently washed with PBS three times. The fresh TSB solutions containing FA-CDs (the final concentrations were 0–1000 μg mL−1) were added to the pre-prepared biofilm, continuing to incubate for 24 h. Upon completion co-cultures, the biofilm attached to the coverslips were carefully washed with PBS to remove metabolic impurities and planktonic bacteria. 1) The biofilms were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 24 h, and dehydrated with 30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 90%, 95% and 100% ethanol solutions. Then, the samples were vacuum freeze-dried. After sputtering coated with gold, SEM was used to observe the morphological changes of the biofilm. 2) After staining with an AO/EB Dual Fluorescence Staining Kit (live/dead staining kit) for 15 min in darkness, Confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, the excitation wavelengths were 488 and 520 nm) was used to detect the survival rate of bacteria in the biofilm. 3) Put the mixed solution in a 96-well plate for culture, and the remaining conditions were kept the same as above. Biofilm on coverslips were fixed in methanol for 15 min. After removing the methanol, the sample was dried at 60 °C. Then, 0.1% crystal violet solution was added to each well for 15 min and washed three times to remove excess dye. Next, after drying at 60 °C, add 200 μL of absolute ethanol to each well and shake for 30 min. An microplate reader (YETO HBS-1101) was used to measure the absorbance of the eluent at 570 nm to calculate the destruction rate. Each test was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting the oxidase-like activity by monitoring ROS generation [45,46]
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous reports, ascorbic acid (AA) has a maximum absorption peak at 266 nm and after oxidation by ROS to dehydroascorbic acid, the absorption peak disappears. Using AA as an indicator, we measured the oxidase-like activity of FA-CDs by monitoring the production of ROS in solution. Specifically, sample solutions of different concentrations were prepared by dissolving FA-CDs in PBS (pH = 5.5 or 7.4). Then, 100 μL of ascorbic acid solution (final concentration 0.2 mM) was added to the FA-CDs solution, keeping the volume of the resulting mixed solution at 2 mL. In this case, the negative control was replaced by PBS and the positive control was replaced by H2O2 (final concentration 10 mM). The mixture was shaken at 37 °C for 4 h. The UV–Vis absorbance of the resulting mixture was measured using a UV–Vis spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, Evolution 220) in the range of 200–450 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting the peroxidase (POD)-like activity of FA-CDs [47]
ce:section.ce:para: In the HAC-NaAc buffer solution containing hydrogen peroxide, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzylidine (TMB) can be oxidized to TMB+ by nanomaterials with peroxidase activity. The solution turns dark green and a new absorption appears at 652 nm. Typically, FA-CDs were dissolved in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 5.5 or 7.4) to obtain the sample solutions of different concentrations. Then, 50 μL of TMB solution (final concentration 0.5 mM) and 100 μL of H2O2 (final concentration 0.5 M) were sequentially added to above solution and incubated in shaker at 37 °C for 10 min. The absorbance of different mixtures were measured at 652 nm using the microplate reader (YETO HBS-1101) at 652 nm to verify the POD-like activity of the sample. The buffer solution without sample was used as the negative control group. Each sample was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting the total superoxide dismutase (SOD) activities of S. aureus [33]
ce:section.ce:para: The total SOD activities in bacterial cells was detected by a typical nitrogen blue tetrazolium (NBT) colorimetric method. Superoxide anion (O2 ·-) reduces NBT to blue formazan, which has maximum absorption peak at 560 nm. SOD can scavenge O2 ·- and inhibit the formation of formazan. The lighter the blue of the reaction solution, the higher the activities of SOD. Therefore, the SOD activities can be evaluated by colorimetric analysis. Specifically, 20 mL of S. aureus suspension (∼106 CFU mL−1) was centrifuged and washed 2–3 times with PBS. The cells were disrupted using an ultrasonic cell pulverizer after pre-cooled in an ice bath. Then, the homogenate was centrifuged at 4 °C and the samples were dissolved in the collected supernatant for next testing. Finally, 20 μL of the solution to be tested, 160 μL of NBT/enzyme working solution (containing 158 μL of SOD detection buffer, 1 μL of NBT and 1 μL of enzyme solution) and 20 μL reaction start working solution (containing 19.5 μL of SOD detection buffer and 0.5 μL reaction starting solution) were mixed in a 96-well plate, and incubated in a shaker at 37 °C for 30 min. The absorbance of the mixed solution was measured at 560 nm using an microplate reader. The supernatant without the samples was used as the control group, and its inhibition rate of total SOD activity was 0. Each sample was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting the total intracellular ROS of S. aureus [48]
ce:section.ce:para: Using DCFH-DA fluorescent stain as an indicator, intracellular ROS levels induced by FA-CDs was detected by a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi). Specifically, 2 mL of S. aureus suspension (∼106 CFU mL−1) was mixed with 4 mL of DCFH-DA (20 mM), and incubated in a shaker at 37 °C for 120 min. Subsequently, 2 mL of different sample solutions dispersed in PBS (final concentration 1 mg mL−1) were added to the mixed solution, continuing to incubate in shaker at 37 °C. The negative control group was replaced by PBS, and the positive control group was replaced by H2O2 (final concentration 10 mM). At a specific incubation time (1, 2, 4, 6, 8h), 1 mL of the mixed solution was taken out for testing. Finally, fluorescence was measured at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm and an emission wavelength of 500–700 nm. The intracellular ROS levels was proportional to the fluorescent intensity. Each sample was repeated three times.

The in vitro cell cytotoxicity was evaluated through the viability of mouse embryonic fibroblast cell (NIH-3T3) by employing the CCK-8 assay [49]. NIH-3T3 cells were cultured in fetal bovine serum and antibiotic solution supplemented Eagle medium at 37 °C for 24 h. Samples (final concentration 0, 125, 250, 500, 1000 and 2000 μg mL−1) were co-incubated with prepared cells for 1, 3 or 5 days. The treated cells were then seeded in 96-well plates and incubated with 10 μL of CCK-8 solution (2 mg mL−1) for 4 h. Finally, the cell viability relative to the blank control group were calculated by measuring the absorbance at 450 nm with a microplate reader. Each experiment was repeated five times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Live/dead staining of cells treated with FA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental cells were mouse embryonic fibroblasts cell(NIH-3T3), and the cell culture medium was fresh DMEM medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin-streptomycin. FA-CDs solution of different concentrations and NIH-3T3 cells were seeded in 96-well plates (1 × 104 cells per well) and incubated in 200 μL of cell culture medium for 24 h. After incubation, the medium was removed and cells were washed twice with PBS (pH 7.4). Then, Live/Dead staining solution (100 μL) was added to each well and stained at room temperature for 6 h in the dark. Finally, fluorescent images of cell morphology were observed with an inverted fluorescence microscope. The control group was the cells without CDs treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound healing on the backs of mice [33,38]
ce:section.ce:para: We established an infectious wound model on the back of mice in order to verify the antibacterial effect of FA-CDs in vivo. 15 healthy male mice (6 weeks, 18–22 g) were randomly divided into three groups (treated with stroke-physiological saline solution, treated with FA-CD280, and treated with cefquinoxime sulfate). An oval wound of approximately 15 mm2 (6 mm*4 mm) was created on the back, and 10 μl of S. aureus (∼108 CFU mL−1) was added dropwise to the wound. After the wound model was established, 10 μl of stroke-physiological saline solution, FA-CD280 solution (1 mg mL−1) or cefquinome sulfate solution(10 mg mL−1) were added dropwise to the wounds, respectively. Subsequently, each group of mice were individually raised in cages at standard temperature (24–26 °C). The wounds were observed and photographed every 24 h and the area of infected skin during treatment was calculated using ImageJ software. To quantify the bacteria in the wound tissue treated with different drugs, the wound tissue was homogenized in stroke-physiological saline solution on the first and fourth day after treatment. The diluted homogenate were coated on LB solid medium for incubation. Colonies were counted after 24 h of incubation at 37 °C. After 4 d of treatment, all mice were sacrificed, and the wounds and surrounding skin tissue were excised and stored in 4% paraformaldehyde to prepare pathological sections. The sections were stained by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and analyzed histologically. The animal experiment was approved by the Experimental Animal Ethics Committee of 920th Hospital of Joint Logistics Support Force, and in accordance with the guidelines of the animals care and use committee.

All data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation. All experimental records were from at least three parallel experiments. t-test (heteroscedastic, 2-tails) was used to evaluate statistical significance between the statistical data. p values of less than 0.05 were considered to be significant.

A series of FA-CDs were synthesized by a concise pyrolysis method from folic acid, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Fig. 1 A showed the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of FA-CD280. It can be seen that FA-CDs were quasi-spherical with a graphite structure and displayed the intercrystalline spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite [50]. Statistical analysis extracted from HRTEM image exhibited the average particle size of 2.0–2.5 nm. As shown in Fig. 1B, the mean hydrodynamic size measured by DLS was approximately 2.8 nm [51]. Next, UV–Vis absorption spectra of FA and FA-CDs were recorded. As shown in Fig. 1C, the absorption peaks of FA-CDs were significantly blue-shifted compared to FA. The absorption peaks at 250–300 nm, showing the electronic transition π→π*, were intrinsic to the aromatic sp [2] mixing region, which originated from the core of the CDs formed during pyrolysis and the structure of benzene ring contained in the raw. The absorption peak near 325 nm, which marked the electronic transition n→π* of FA-CDs32,33, was attributed to surface and edge defects of the carbon dots. The absorption peaks of FA-CDs synthesized at different temperatures had the same absorption band, however, the absorption intensities varied, which demonstrated different carbon cores and surface chemical states.

The functional groups on surface were important factor affecting the antimicrobial properties of the CDs; therefore, FTIR and XPS were carried out. Fig. 1D showed the FTIR spetra of FA and the as-prepared FA-CDs. Among them, the peak at 1691 cm−1 was the stretching vibration peak of CO, and near 1487 cm−1 was the characteristic peak of CC in the pteridine ring [41,42,52,54]. After pyrolysis, the peak of CO and CC were significantly weakened and CC near 1487 cm−1 was shifted to 1524 cm−1. These changes indicated that some structure of FA were partially destroyed during the process of pyrolysis. Meanwhile, the peaks of CN near 1377 cm−1 for FA-CDs were obviously enhanced, suggesting an increase of new nitrogen-containing carbon rings. Other groups, such as O–H and N–H (3025–3500 cm−1), still appeared in CDs prepared at different temperatures. Based on above mentioned, we speculated that, during the formation of CDs, the core structure were composed of new nitrogen-containing carbon rings formed by the condensation between FA molecules, while some functional groups of FA remained on the surface of FA-CDs [50]. For the characterization of N-doped carbon-based nanomaterials, XPS was a powerful technique which can distinguish the local electronic structures of the doped-N and carbon atoms, so the surface elemental composition of the FA-CDs prepared at 250 °C, 280 °C, 310 °C was tested and analyzed by XPS [55,56]. Fig. 1E indicated that the elemental contents (atomic percentages) of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen were 68.42%, 13.66%, and 17.92% for FA-CD280, respectively. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s, the peak with a binding energy of 284.8 eV demonstrated the presence of CN/CC (aromatic ring, 48.7%). The peaks at 285.8, 288.2 eV belonged to C–C/C–N (35.8%) and OC–OH (15.5%), respectively [57,58]. The N1s spectrum was consisted of three peaks at 399.3, 400.2 and 401.1 eV, associated with aromatic ring N (i.e. pyridinic N, 47.5%), graphite N (37.0%) and N–H (15.5%) [42,58,59]. The O1s spectrum contained two peaks at 531.6 and 533.4 eV, corresponding to CO (83.3%) and C–O (17.7%), respectively [42,52,59]. Furthermore, the change in nitrogen content of the CDs prepared for 250 °C, 280 °C, and 310 °C was obvious with 6.76%, 13.66%, and 8.41%, respectively (Fig. S2). The percentage of different types of N atoms, aromatic ring N, graphitic N, and amino N, varied little, where the content of aromatic ring N was 48.11%, 47.49%, and 49.41%, respectively. From the above analysis, it was found that FA-CDs formed nitrogen-containing carbon rings similar to the graphitic structure, coinciding with the results of FTIR, UV and HRTEM, which would be essential for their antibacterial properties. The FA-CDs prepared at 280 °C had the highest N-doping content, and also the highest pyridinic N content. We speculate that this may be due to the low nucleation rate of hetero-element-doped CDs when the temperature was lower than 280 °C; when the temperature was higher than 280 °C, the graphitic network of CDs went in the direction of producing fewer defects, resulting in a lower N content [56].

XRD was used to further investigate the crystalline structure of FA-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1F, the high intensity of the X-ray diffraction peaks of folic acid indicated that the raw material had an ordered microstructure. After calcination, only a broad diffraction peak remained in the XRD spectrum of FA-CDs. In addition, we recorded the fluorescence emission spectra of FA-CDs prepared at different temperatures (Figs. 1G,S3,S4). The intensity of the fluorescence emission peaks of FA-CD280 enhanced and then weakened with the variation of excitation wavelength from 320–440 nm, and the maximum emission spectrum was obtained at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The maximum fluorescence emission peak remained around 430 nm at different excitation wavelengths. The excitation-independent emission may arise from the uniform edge-state (n-π*) transition of the CO bond [25,50]. The relative homogeneity of CDs surface leads to a narrow distribution of surface energy states [53].

MIC testings in neutral or mild acidic environments were carried out to evaluate the antimicrobial activity of FA-CDs synthesized at various temperatures ranging from 250 to 310 °C. As can be seen from Table 1 and S1, the raw material was devoid of antimicrobial activity. Strikingly, CDs derived from folic acid (FA-CDs) exhibited antibacterial activity in the mild acidic environment and had no antibacterial activity in the neutral environment. Moreover, we found an interesting phenomenon that FA-CDs showed selective killing for S. aureus, while it almost didn't have toxic effect on E. coli. At pH 5.5, the inhibitory activity of FA-CDs for S. aureus increased as the increasing of synthesis temperature from 250 to 280 °C, and then decreased. Among them, FA-CD280 showed the highest antibacterial activity with a MIC value of 16 μg mL−1. As we all know, the pH value of normal sites was neutral, but the local environment at the bacterial infection sites became mild acidic [13–17], which means that FA-CDs can selectively kill S. aureus at the infection sites, while didn't exhibit antibacterial activity in normal tissues. The pH-responsive antibacterial ability of FA-CDs would facilitate their practical biocidal applications and reduce the production of drug-resistant bacteria [33].

The antibacterial efficiency of FA-CDs was further studied by time-dynamic bactericidal assay. As shown in Fig. 2 A,2B, S5, the bacterial survivals at different times were determined in the mild acidic environment. The substrate untouched with FA-CDs was used as a control group, taking the survival rate of bacteria as 100%. During the co-cultivation of S. aureus with FA-CDs, the number of the viable colonies was gradually and distinctly reduced as contact time extends. FA-CD280, which possessed the best antibacterial effect, killed 80% of S.aureus after 4 h of contact, and killed almost all bacteria after 6 h. However, all FA-CDs showed no antibacterial activity against E. coli (Fig. S5). Therefore, FA-CD280 with excellent antibacterial activity in an acidic environment will have promising potential in the field of preventing S. aureus infections.

Next, the morphological changes of bacterial cells before and after FA-CD280 treatment were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Before the contact of S. aureus and E. coli with FA-CDs, the morphology of bacteria was full, the structure was intact, and the surface was smooth (Figs. 2C and S6A). After co-cultivating with FA-CD280 for 4 h at acid environment, the bacterial body of S. aureus (Fig. 2D) aggregated and deflated(marked by yellow arrow) significantly, while small holes (marked by green arrow) appeared on the surface and a small amount of cytoplasmic contents flowed out of some bacterial bodies (marked by red arrow). In contrast, there were almost no change in E. coli (Fig. S6B). It was speculated that due to the different cell membrane structures of S. aureus and E. coli, FA-CDs were more likely to aggregate on the surface of S. aureus, leading to disruption of the cell membrane structure.

It was well known that a large number of bacteria grew and aggregated numerously inside the biofilm and their acidic metabolites made the local microenvironment acidic, meanwhile, folate receptors was overexpressed on the surface of biofilm. Therefore, we envisaged that the pH-responsive antibacterial FA-CDs with residual groups of folic acid may have potential application in the field of antibiofilm.

Bacterial biofilms community were protected by EPS matrix against the penetration of exogenous antimicrobial agents, making it difficult to effectively kill bacteria inside the mature biofilms. As a potential candidate for biofilm removal, we systematically evaluated the antibiofilm performance of FA-CDs. The destructive ability against mature biofilms was quantitatively determined by crystalline violet staining assay. As seen from Fig. 3 A, FA-CDs exhibited significant antibiofilm activity. With the increase of pyrolysis temperature, the ability of disrupting mature biofilms increased and then decreased, among which, FA-CD280 (1000 μg mL−1) showed the strongest antibiofilm capacity, up to 82%, while the destruction rate was negligible for FA. Meanwhile, we also examined the effect of different FA-CD280 concentrations on the destruction of mature biofilms. It was obvious that the destruction rate increased with the concentration of FA-CD280. Notably, the disruption rate remained at a high level (61%) even treated with a low concentration of 32 μg mL−1, confirming its superior antibiofilm ability.

Next, SEM was utilized to observe the morphology and structure of S. aureus biofilms before and after treated with FA-CD280. As seen from the SEM images (Fig. 3B), the control group showed intact and dense biofilms. The morphology of the biofilm treated with FA-CDs was greatly altered and became sparse and loose, exhibiting a fragmentation-like state. The destructive degree to biofilm increased significantly with the increase of FA-CDs. When the concentration reached 1000 μg mL−1, the bacteria basically showed a free state, indicating that the biofilm was almost completely disrupted.

To further investigate the antibiofilm activity of FA-CD280 and the survival rate of bacteria inside the biofilm, the three-dimensional images of S. aureus biofilm treated with different concentrations of FA-CD280 were observed by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) after staining the biofilm with AO/EB stain. As shown in Fig. 3B, the untreated mature biofilm grew well and formed a dense appearance. Almost no red fluorescence was observed in the biofilm, and the bacteria were mostly in a viable state. After treatment with 32 μg mL−1 of FA-CD280, the green fluorescence was significantly reduced, indicating that the structural integrity of the biofilm was disrupted and bacteria was killed. The red fluorescence began to appear in the visual field, but there were still many bacteria living in it. As the concentration gradually increased, the thinness and looseness of the biofilm structure were obviously observed

and the survival rate of bacteria decreased. When the concentration reached 1000 μg mL−1, the bacteria basically showed red fluorescence, demonstrating that bacteria inside biofilm were almost completely killed and the mature biofilm was destroyed, which was in good agreement with the experimental results of crystalline violet staining experiment and SEM. As mentioned above, it was indicated that FA-CDs can effectively remove mature biofilms, which made it hold a promising potential in the field of antibacterial infection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of antibacterial and antibiofilm activity
ce:section.ce:para: As the natural form of water-soluble vitamins, FA gives no antimicrobial activity. However, CDs derived from FA exhibited excellent and distinctive bactericidal activity with pH responsiveness and selectivity, which was worth exploring in detail.

The antibacterial results showed that FA-CDs were effective in killing S. aureus, but had no effect on E. coli. Here we mixed S. aureus and E. coli suspensions of different concentrations with FA-CD280. After incubation for 4 h, the mixtures were centrifuged to collect the supernatants, and measured their fluorescence intensity. From Fig. 4 A, we can see that the fluorescence intensity of the solution mixed with S. aureus obviously decreased, while the one mixed with E. coli showed almost no change at all. The result clearly indicated that FA-CDs bound more readily to S. aureus, which would be related to the different cell membrane structure of Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria [60]. It was well known that the cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus was composed of a simple structural monolayer of peptidoglycan and teichoic acid [60]. After co-culture with S. aureus, FA-CDs easily accumulated in the porous cell wall due to their ultra-small size. In contrast to Gram-positive bacteria, the cell wall contained a peptidoglycan layer between the protective outer cell membrane and the cytoplasmic membrane. This unique structure can effectively reduce the penetration of FA-CDs and avoid accumulation onto the inner membrane of E. coli. Next, we investigated how the FA-CDs bound to bacterial cells kill S. aureus.

Generally, the cationic surface was one of the common mechanisms contributing to the antimicrobial activity of carbon dots. Many nitrogen-doped CDs were positively charged, prompting the CDs to interact with negatively charged bacterial cell membranes, which can disrupt the integrity of the cell membranes and interfere with the normal physiological metabolism of bacteria, thereby killing them [26,32,37,38,61,62]. The results of Zeta potential(Fig. 4B) showed that FA and all as-prepared FA-CDs exhibited negative charges. It was generally known that FA exhibited negative electrical properties since it contained a large number of negatively charged groups. Based on the active structure retention mechanism, FA-CDs retained part of the folate groups and also exhibited negative electrical charge [28,32]. Considering the negative surfaces of both FA-CDs and bacteria, therefore, the surface charge wasn't the key factor promoting their antibacterial activity.

According to previous reports, the ROS in bacterial cells was regulated by their redox enzyme systems and maintained a dynamic balance. The elevated intracellular ROS levels was considered as one of the main mechanisms causing bacteria death [32–36]. As a zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, CDs showed good enzyme-like activity, which has been widely reported [33–36,63–65]. Oxidase can catalyze oxygen to generate free radicals and promote the increase of ROS levels. Using ascorbic acid as probe, we examined the oxidase-like activity of FA-CDs with monitoring the generation of ROS in the solution system. As shown in Fig. 4C, in the acidic environment of pH 5.5, the absorption peak intensity of ascorbic acid was significantly lower, which implies that FA-CDs are able to increase the level of ROS in environment. However, the ROS levels of FA-CDs in a neutral environment remained almost unchanged. Meanwhile, as the concentration of FA-CDs increased, the total amount of ROS produced in the system gradually rose. Furthermore, peroxidases (POD) can catalyzes the production of hydroxyl radicals, which would increase the oxidation activity of ROS [47,66]. In the presence of H2O2, TMB was oxidized to TMB+ with the catalysis of POD and the solution changed to dark green. As shown in Fig. 4D, after contact with FA-CDs at pH 5.5, the color of the solution became obviously dark green, indicating their outstanding POD-like activity in an acidic environment. It was found that the pyrolysis temperatures had a significant impact on the POD activities of FA-CDs and FA-CD280 possessed the highest activity. Moreover, as the concentration of FA-CDs increased, the POD-like activity also increased accordingly.

The enzyme-like activity of FA-CDs, we speculate, may be related to the carbon core and catalytically active surface sites of the nitrogen-doped CDs, which were readily formed during the process of pyrolytic condensation [35,37]. According to the literature, OXD-like and POD-like activities catalyzed the reactions similar to oxygen reduction reaction [47]. Guo reported that pyridinic N in nitrogen-doped graphitic carbons created the active sites for oxygen reduction reaction under acidic conditions. Carbon atoms next to pyridinic N were suggested to be the active sites at which O2 molecules were adsorbed. Apart from the pyridinic N structure, the carbonation process forming graphitic structure was also important for the enzyme-like activity [47,67]. As gleaned from the XPS results, FA-CDs prepared at 280 °C had the highest pyridinic N content, therefore had more active catalytic sites for oxygen reduction reaction, thus exhibiting stronger catalytic activity [67]. This results were consistent with the experiment results probing the OXD-like and POD-like activities of FA-CDs, which further verify the rationality of our speculation. In-depth understanding is still required to identify the roles of CDs’ structures in the catalysis of enzyme-like activities.

Superoxide dismutase (SOD), as the cellular antioxidant defense system, was capable of reducing superoxide and decrease the intracellular ROS level, protecting cells against oxidative damage [33,47,67]. To further investigate the ability of FA-CDs to regulate ROS, we measured the total activity of SOD in bacterial cells with S. aureus as the experimental subject. As shown in Fig. 4E, similarly, FA-CDs prepared at various temperatures exhibited different abilities to inhibit SOD activities. FA-CD280 showed the most pronounced inhibition capacity on SOD activity of S. aureus, and the inhibition percentage rose to 90% with increasing FA-CDs concentration.

Finally, we examined the total levels of ROS in S. aureus cells treated with different samples using DCFH-DA as a probe. In the acidic environment mimicking infected tissues, FA-CDs were able to significantly increase the levels of intracellular ROS. Obviously, the ROS levels induced by FA-CDs prepared at 270–290 °C were significantly higher than the positive control group (H2O2, 10 mM). Meanwhile, taking FA-CD280 as the example, the relationship between the intracellular ROS levels and exposure time was examined, and these results were summarized in Fig. 4F. The intracellular ROS levels increased progressively as the exposure time was extended, which matched well with the result from time-dynamic bactericidal assay. After 8 h of exposure, the ROS levels was approximately twice of the positive control group, suggesting excellent bactericidal effect. The intracellular ROS levels no longer increased when the exposure time reached 16 h, which might be related to the massive death of bacteria with increasing exposure time.

Based on the above results and analysis, we can arrive at the following probable smart antibacterial and antibiofilm mechanisms for single FA-CDs nanosystem. Based on the active structure retention mechanism [28], the residual folate groups on the surface of FA-CDs allow them to bind specifically to folate receptors overexpressed by the bacterial biofilm, in favor of the enrichment of FA-CDs at the infection site. Next, the ultra-small size of FA-CDs facilitate their entry inside biofilms and interaction with bacterial cells in the deep biofilm. Only in the acidic environment of infected sites, FA-CDs can exhibited excellent oxidase-like and peroxidase-like activities, which could gradually increase the intracellular ROS level. On the other hand, they can inhibit the activity of SOD in bacterial cells, leading to a weakened ability of bacteria to scavenge excessive ROS. Both of these aspects, together, lead to the disruption of the ROS balance in the bacterial organism, which inactive a variety of proteins, degrade nucleic acids, disrupt the integrity of cell membranes, and thus break down extracellular polymeric substances, for killing bacteria and removing bacterial biofilms. In addition, we can conjecture the selective antibacterial activity of FA-CDs for S. aureus. The aforementioned results depicted the accumulation of FA-CDs on cytoplasmic membrane of S. aureus, which could increase the level of ROS near the cell membrane, disrupting the cell membrane structure and eventually leading to bacteria death. This conjecture was supported by morphological changes in bacterial cells before and after contact with FA-CD280. The cell membrane of S. aureus after co-incubation was significantly disrupted, in contrast to E. coli, which still had a relatively intact cell structure (Figs. 2C,2D, S6A,S6B).

Cell viability was measured using cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) to evaluate the side effect of FA-CD280 on mammalian cells. Fig. 5 A showed the relative cell viability of NIH-3T3 cells co-cultured with FA-CD280 for 1, 3, 5 days, respectively. The experimental results demonstrated that the relative cell viability was around 100% for the concentrations in the range of 0–500 μg mL−1. As the concentration increased to 1000 μg mL−1, the relative cell viability was slightly reduced, but stayed at above 75%, indicating the excellent biocompatibility of FA-CDs. In addition, the toxicity of FA-CDs to NIH-3T3 fibroblasts was further investigated by live/dead staining assay, and the results were shown in Fig. 5B. Compared with the control group, there didn't have inactivated NIH-3T3 cells in the fluorescent images of the experimental group. Even if the concentration of FA-CD280 was as high as 1000 μg mL−1, the cells in the field of view remained in normal morphology with good cell viability. When the concentration reached 2000 μg mL−1, the morphology of NIH-3T3 cells changed significantly and loss of cell viability became evident, but the cells in the view field were still alive. This indicated that FA-CDs had no obvious cytotoxic effect on mammalian cells at appropriate concentrations accompanied with potential for practical applications in biomedical and public health fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antibacterial ability and wound healing application
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infection at the wound site would hinder wound healing process and even lead to severe consequences. Herein, we established a mouse model of S. aureus-infected back wounds in order to verify the antibacterial effect of FA-CD280 in vivo. Fig. 6 A was the macroscopic views of wounds in the back skin of mice from the each groups during the therapeutic process. After infected with S. aureus for 4 days, the wounds managed without antibacterials agents (just treated with stroke-physiological saline solution) remained edematous. In contrast, FA-CD280 and antibiotic cefquinoxime sulfate had a significant healing-promoting effect on wounds infected with S. aureus. By the fourth day of the experiment, the wounds infected with S. aureus were almost healed. To more clearly demonstrate the change of wound size, we tracked and quantified the change in size of the infected skin area over time of mice in each treatment group(Figs. 6B and S8).

Next, we investigated the in vivo antibacterial effect of FA-CDs at the site of bacterial infection. Fig. 6C demonstrated that there was a significant reduction in the number of S. aureus in both FA-CD280 and Cefquinoxime sulfate-treated wounds compared to the control group for 1 or 4 days. The results suggested that FA-CD280, like Cefquinoxime sulfate, was persistent in the treatment of bacterial infections. After 4 days of treatment, the healed wound was stained with H&E for histological analyses, and the results were shown in Fig. 6D. The epithelium and dermis of the control group treated with Stroke-physiological saline solution had not yet grown solid, and there existed a large area of unrepaired tissue. Moreover, a large number of inflammatory cells had accumulated at the wound site, indicating that the infection caused by bacteria had not been adequately treated and the situation was still serious [38]. In the positive control group treated with Cefquinoxime sulfate, the wound was largely healed, but a large number of inflammatory cells were still present. In contrast, the wound site treated with FA-CD280 contained only a small number of inflammatory cells. Tight connective and follicular tissues formed on the surface of the originally infected wounds, indicating that the FA-CDs treated group clearly manifested superior wound healing. The above results indicated that FA-CD280 show significant antibacterial activity in vivo, and its effect on the treatment of infections caused by S. aureus was equivalent to that of the fourth-generation antibiotic cefquinome sulfate. At the same time, FA-CD280 had excellent biocompatibility, which can help in wound healing and inflammatory cell removal. These characteristics demonstrated that FA-CDs had a tremendous potential to be used clinically in the treatment of S. aureus-infected wounds.

In summary, we have successfully prepared FA-CDs by a simple one-step pyrolysis method using folic acid as raw material. FA-CDs can be activated in the mild acidic environment of the infection sites and exhibit excellent oxidase-like and peroxidase-like activities, which can effectively increase ROS levels, killing S. aureus and removing its mature biofilm. Meanwhile, FA-CDs have no obvious toxicity to free bacteria and normal cells with a neutral condition, reducing the generation of bacterial resistance and side effects in antibacterial therapy. The in vivo experiments demonstrate that FA-CDs have good therapeutic effect on the S. aureus-infected back wound models of mouse without any adverse effects. Considering the simple preparation procedure, distinctive bactericidal ability and biocompatibility, FA-CDs, as an ultra-succinct smart antibacterial nanosystem with pH-responsiveness and selectivity for S. aureus, have a promising potential to become an effective tool in future clinical medicine against S. aureus caused infections.
